findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a well-defined, unilocular or multiloculated cystic mass originating in the sublingual space and extending inferiorly into the submandibular space. The lesion follows fluid attenuation on CT and demonstrates characteristic fluid signal intensity on MRI, appearing hypointense on T1-weighted images and markedly hyperintense on T2-weighted sequences.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the classic 'tail sign', representing the cystic component herniating around the posterior free edge of the mylohyoid muscle to connect the sublingual and submandibular compartments. The cyst is typically thin-walled without internal septations, and does not demonstrate central enhancement.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of an ill-defined, solid enhancing soft tissue mass or necrotic cervical lymphadenopathy that would indicate a primary malignancy. Furthermore, there is no thick irregular rim enhancement or extensive surrounding inflammatory fat stranding to suggest a severe acute infectious process like Ludwig's angina.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the fluid-signal cystic mass communicating between the sublingual and submandibular spaces around the posterior margin of the mylohyoid muscle.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Ranula (plunging), given the unilocular fluid-attenuation mass extending below the mylohyoid muscle. These are mucous retention cysts of the sublingual salivary glands that lack a true epithelial wall and classically present as a painless swelling in the floor of the mouth.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), however this would typically present as an ill-defined, solid, enhancing mass rather than a pure fluid collection. Furthermore, SCC is strongly associated with tobacco use and frequently exhibits regional necrotic lymph node metastases, which are entirely absent in this case.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Infection/abscess, given the potential for rapid progression to life-threatening airway compromise, particularly in the context of Ludwig's angina. However, an acute abscess or cellulitis typically presents with thick, irregular rim enhancement, extensive surrounding inflammatory changes, and a clinical history of pain and fever often stemming from a dental origin.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately map the anatomical extent of the lesion in relation to the mylohyoid muscle and adjacent neurovascular structures to assist surgical planning.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is helpful to confirm the slow-growing nature of the cyst and to identify any prior episodes of spontaneous rupture or superinfection.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the neck if only a CT or ultrasound has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the fluid nature of the cyst, mapping its relationship to the mylohyoid muscle, and confirming the absence of solid nodular components.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted sequences with fat saturation to optimally demonstrate the hyperintense fluid tracking into the submandibular space, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to confidently exclude any solid enhancing tumor and assess for peripheral enhancement that might indicate superinfection.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Head and Neck / Maxillofacial Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the origin in the sublingual space and the exact path of extension into the submandibular space to guide complete surgical excision of both the plunging cyst and the ipsilateral sublingual gland to prevent recurrence.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a well-defined, unilocular or multiloculated cystic mass originating in the sublingual space and extending inferiorly into the submandibular space. The lesion follows fluid attenuation on CT and demonstrates characteristic fluid signal intensity on MRI, appearing hypointense on T1-weighted images and markedly hyperintense on T2-weighted sequences. Supporting features include the classic 'tail sign', representing the cystic component herniating around the posterior free edge of the mylohyoid muscle to connect the sublingual and submandibular compartments. The cyst is typically thin-walled without internal septations, and does not demonstrate central enhancement. Importantly, there is no evidence of an ill-defined, solid enhancing soft tissue mass or necrotic cervical lymphadenopathy that would indicate a primary malignancy. Furthermore, there is no thick irregular rim enhancement or extensive surrounding inflammatory fat stranding to suggest a severe acute infectious process like Ludwig's angina. The feature that most influences my thinking is the fluid-signal cystic mass communicating between the sublingual and submandibular spaces around the posterior margin of the mylohyoid muscle.

The appearances are most in keeping with Ranula (plunging), given the unilocular fluid-attenuation mass extending below the mylohyoid muscle. These are mucous retention cysts of the sublingual salivary glands that lack a true epithelial wall and classically present as a painless swelling in the floor of the mouth. The principle differential would be Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), however this would typically present as an ill-defined, solid, enhancing mass rather than a pure fluid collection. Furthermore, SCC is strongly associated with tobacco use and frequently exhibits regional necrotic lymph node metastases, which are entirely absent in this case. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Infection/abscess, given the potential for rapid progression to life-threatening airway compromise, particularly in the context of Ludwig's angina. However, an acute abscess or cellulitis typically presents with thick, irregular rim enhancement, extensive surrounding inflammatory changes, and a clinical history of pain and fever often stemming from a dental origin.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately map the anatomical extent of the lesion in relation to the mylohyoid muscle and adjacent neurovascular structures to assist surgical planning. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is helpful to confirm the slow-growing nature of the cyst and to identify any prior episodes of spontaneous rupture or superinfection. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the neck if only a CT or ultrasound has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the fluid nature of the cyst, mapping its relationship to the mylohyoid muscle, and confirming the absence of solid nodular components. I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted sequences with fat saturation to optimally demonstrate the hyperintense fluid tracking into the submandibular space, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to confidently exclude any solid enhancing tumor and assess for peripheral enhancement that might indicate superinfection. This should go to the Head and Neck / Maxillofacial Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the origin in the sublingual space and the exact path of extension into the submandibular space to guide complete surgical excision of both the plunging cyst and the ipsilateral sublingual gland to prevent recurrence.
